description: Climate science and policy governance domain. Real-world decisions at the IPCC and COP negotiations — where calibrated scientific consensus meets political authority. Structural parallels with fukushima_governance: expert evidence repeatedly acknowledged then discounted by governance bodies under economic pressure.
grounding_notes: IPCC AR6 is the authoritative scientific assessment (2021). COP decisions are official UNFCCC documents. Stern Review (2006) for discount rate analysis. Tipping threshold ranges from Lenton et al. 2019 (Nature).

attributes.role: Former NASA GISS director, climate scientist
attributes.note: 1988 Congressional testimony — first major public attribution of anthropogenic warming. His 350ppm target was adopted; his warnings about glacier loss and sea level have been consistently validated.
attributes.dissent_history: Repeatedly criticized IPCC assessments as too conservative, and COP commitments as insufficient

attributes.role: Chief Scientist, The Nature Conservancy; Professor, Texas Tech
attributes.note: Specializes in making climate risk tangible at regional and community level. High communication calibration.
AR6 (2021) declared 'code red for humanity'. Historically assessments have been politically conservative due to consensus requirement across 195 member governments.
Declared Glasgow 'a turning point'. Post-analysis by Climate Action Tracker: pledges put world on track for 2.4°C — well above 1.5°C target.
Independent scientific assessment. Consistently more pessimistic than official COP communications — and consistently closer to subsequent observed data.

attributes.uncertainty_range: 300–900 GtCO2 depending on non-CO2 forcing assumptions

attributes.full_name: Special Report on Global Warming of 1.5°C
attributes.key_finding: Limiting warming to 1.5°C requires 45% CO2 reduction by 2030 and net-zero by 2050
attributes.political_reception: Acknowledged by all COP parties; NDCs submitted post-SR1.5 collectively projected 2.7°C

attributes.key_concession: 'Phase down' instead of 'phase out' coal — India and China demanded
attributes.loss_and_damage: No finance mechanism agreed — deferred to 2022

attributes.claim: Global warming is here, is caused by human activity, and poses serious risks
attributes.reception: Unprecedented media coverage; Reagan/Bush administrations did not alter policy
attributes.retrospective_accuracy: All three scenarios presented were broadly correct; warming slightly above middle scenario

attributes.description: Difference between committed NDCs and emissions reductions needed for 1.5°C
attributes.equivalent_to: Current annual US + EU + China emissions combined

attributes.definition: Thresholds beyond which self-reinforcing feedbacks drive further warming independent of human action
attributes.ipcc_assessment: Some tipping points possible below 2°C; cascades possible above 2°C
attributes.note: Tipping point risk is consistently excluded from official cost-benefit analyses due to difficulty of quantification

mechanisms.name: Political Time-Horizon Discounting
mechanisms.notes: Governments systematically underweight long-horizon climate costs vs. short-horizon political costs. Well-documented in policy literature.

decisions.question: Should the Glasgow Pact commit to 'phase out' or 'phase down' unabated coal?
decisions.outcome: Phase down — India and China objection accepted in final hours
decisions.rationale: India's Environment Minister stated phase-out language unfair to developing nations. UK COP Presidency accepted 'phase down' to secure agreement rather than lose consensus.
decisions.notes: Science said phase-out was necessary. Authority said phase-down was politically achievable. Pattern matches fukushima_governance seawall decision.

dissents.rationale: Glasgow was a failure. We're in a situation where politicians and fossil fuel industry representatives can go to Glasgow and say we're going to keep warming below 1.5°C, while planning to open new oil fields. The gap between rhetoric and reality has never been wider.

evidence.content_ref: Climate Action Tracker (2021) - Glasgow's 2030 credibility gap: net zero's lip service to climate action
evidence.notes: Post-Glasgow assessment: NDC pledges consistent with 2.4°C, not 1.5°C

evidence.content_ref: IPCC AR6 Working Group I (2021) - Climate Change 2021: The Physical Science Basis

bridge_candidates.claim: The COP26 'phase down' vs 'phase out' compromise is structurally identical to the TEPCO seawall decision: governance bodies acknowledge expert scientific evidence then discount it under political/economic pressure
bridge_candidates.structural_loss: COP26 was a multilateral negotiation across 195 states; TEPCO seawall was a single corporate decision. Scale and reversibility differ. Pattern of evidence-to-policy discount is identical.
